statement.en: Polyunsaturated fatty acids mediate the antitumor effects of fasting by inducing lipid peroxidation and ferroptosis in breast cancer
statement.summary: Published preclinical studies showed that cyclic fasting or fasting-mimicking diets (FMDs) delay tumour progression and enhance survival in murine models of breast cancer. Our lipidomic analyses revealed that both fasting and FMDs lead to elevated plasma levels of the polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) arachidonic acid (AA) and docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) in mice and in patients with breast cancer. Gene set enrichment analysis of transcriptomic profiles from breast cancer samples of patients subjected to cyclic FMD showed a pronounced activation of fatty acid metabolism–related pathways. Here, we show that the anticancer effects of fasting are rescued if systemic lipolysis is inhibited and plasma fatty acid increase during fasting is prevented. Furthermore, the supplementation of an excess

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The manuscript presents preliminary preclinical and early clinical findings (lipidomic/transcriptomic data) with mechanistic claims about PUFA-mediated ferroptosis in breast cancer. The abstract is truncated, limiting full evaluation. Core claims (fasting → PUFA elevation → ferroptosis → tumor suppression) are biologically plausible but require: (1) completed clinical validation beyond gene set enrichment; (2) causal pathway confirmation (not just correlation); (3) peer review and replication. Published as 2026 future date suggests preprint/provisional status. Mechanistic hypothesis is testable but not yet established fact.
